note: Seed backfill compiled from major public AVSEC events Jul 2025 - May 2026. Best-effort major-event reconstruction, thinner than the live daily pull. The daily Action accumulates fresh data from here.

days.briefing: Scattered Spider's social-engineering campaign sweeps global aviation, hitting carriers across three continents; analysts warn cyberattacks on the sector have surged sharply year-on-year.

items.title: Qantas breach exposes up to 6M customer records
items.summary: Scattered Spider suspected in a breach exposing personal data of up to six million Qantas customers via a third party.

items.title: Scattered Spider hits WestJet and Hawaiian Airlines
items.summary: Coordinated social-engineering attacks disrupted internal systems and apps at North American carriers.

items.title: Air France-KLM third-party platform breached
items.summary: Unauthorised activity detected on an external customer-service platform used by the carriers.

items.title: Thales: aviation cyberattacks up ~600%
items.summary: Thales reported aviation cyberattacks rose roughly 600 percent year-on-year, with dozens of ransomware incidents logged.

days.briefing: A quieter month for incidents but heavy on rule-making: regulators on both sides of the Atlantic tighten aviation cyber obligations.

items.title: TSA issues aviation cybersecurity amendments
items.summary: TSA rolled out amendments mandating stronger cyber controls and incident-reporting protocols for the aviation sector.

items.title: EASA Part-IS information-security rules near deadline
items.summary: EASA Part-IS requirements extend information-security duties to airlines, airports and ANSPs with phased deadlines into 2026.

items.title: ICAO advances Cybersecurity Action Plan
items.summary: ICAO pushed its Cybersecurity Action Plan focusing on governance, faster incident response and security-by-design.

days.briefing: Europe's worst aviation security week in years: a supply-chain ransomware attack paralyses major-hub check-in while a wave of airport drone incursions triggers hybrid-threat fears.

items.title: Collins Aerospace MUSE ransomware grounds check-in
items.summary: HardBit ransomware crippled the Collins MUSE check-in platform, forcing manual processing at Heathrow, Brussels, Berlin and Dublin.

items.title: Copenhagen and Oslo airports shut by drones
items.summary: Large drones forced a four-hour Copenhagen shutdown and a brief Oslo closure; Denmark called it a hybrid attack on critical infrastructure.

items.title: Wave of drone closures across Danish airports
items.summary: Repeated sightings closed Aalborg, Billund and other Danish airports over several nights, with military sites also affected.

items.title: ENISA confirms airport outage was ransomware
items.summary: The EU cyber agency ENISA confirmed the Collins airport disruption as a ransomware attack and opened investigations.

days.briefing: The drone disruption spreads south and west across Europe; governments increasingly frame the incursions as state-linked hybrid activity.

items.title: Munich Airport suspends flights twice over drones
items.summary: Munich halted operations twice within 24 hours over drone sightings, stranding thousands of passengers.

items.title: Brussels and Liege airports closed by drones
items.summary: Belgian airports briefly closed amid sightings; drones also seen over military bases and a nuclear plant.

items.title: Germany assesses drones as likely Russian espionage
items.summary: German officials judged many overflights as probable state-linked espionage aimed at unsettling the public.

days.briefing: Counter-drone response moves from improvisation to coordination, with allied deployments in Europe and a US legal-authority push.

items.title: Allies send counter-drone help to Belgium
items.summary: Near-daily incursions over Belgian airports and bases prompted UK, France and Germany to deploy counter-drone support.

items.title: US drone incursions near airports up 25%+
items.summary: FAA data showed a sharp rise in airport drone incursions, intensifying the counter-UAS authority debate in Congress.

days.briefing: Year-end sees authorities hard-coding the autumn drone lessons into temporary bans and broader take-down powers.

items.title: Denmark and EU impose drone bans around summits
items.summary: Temporary civilian drone bans were placed around airports and critical sites during high-profile EU events.

items.title: US expands counter-UAS take-down authority
items.summary: Congress moved to let more federal, state and local agencies disable rogue drones, subject to training.

days.briefing: The new year opens with drones firmly reframed from nuisance to national-security and business-continuity risk.

items.title: Cartel border drones reframed as security threat
items.summary: Washington's terrorist designation of cartels recast persistent border drone activity as a national-security matter.

items.title: 2026 outlook: drones as a repeatable disruption tactic
items.summary: Analysts warned drone incursions now behave like a low-cost, repeatable business-interruption tactic against critical sites.

days.briefing: Interagency miscoordination over counter-drone lasers shuts a major US airport twice in a month, the most disruptive domestic airspace closures since 9/11.

items.title: FAA abruptly shuts El Paso airspace, then reverses
items.summary: The FAA grounded El Paso flights for a stated 10 days then lifted it hours later, tied to a counter-drone laser test near Fort Bliss.

items.title: Second laser incident closes El Paso airspace again
items.summary: Two weeks later the military downed a CBP drone with a laser, prompting another El Paso closure and congressional criticism.

days.briefing: A quieter month dominated by follow-through: regulators press supply-chain resilience after the autumn ransomware shock.

items.title: Post-Collins aviation supply-chain resilience review
items.summary: Reviews pushed EU supply-chain resilience and NIS2-aligned incident-reporting expectations for aviation technology vendors.

days.briefing: A fatal perimeter breach at a major US hub puts airfield intrusion and perimeter integrity back in the spotlight.

items.title: Denver airport perimeter breach fatality
items.summary: A man breached Denver International's perimeter and was killed on a runway as a jet took off, reviving scrutiny of perimeter security.

days.briefing: Global aviation security remains under significant pressure in mid-2026, with GPS spoofing attacks targeting Indian airports, a fatal perimeter breach at Denver International, and persistent cyber threats against aviation supply chains dominating the threat landscape. Regulatory activity is intensifying, with the FAA advancing long-delayed counter-UAS rulemaking, EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity mandate now fully in force, and ICAO's 2026-2028 Global Aviation Security Plan directing new work on weaponised UAS and GNSS interference.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at seven airports including Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru, delivering false navigation data during landing; no flights cancelled.

items.title: Fatal Perimeter Breach at Denver International Airport Triggers Security Review
items.summary: A man breached Denver Airport's perimeter on 8 May and was killed on an active runway, exposing critical perimeter security gaps.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM, published 6 May 2026, establishes drone flight restriction zones over critical facilities; comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Breached in MFA-Fatigue Phishing Campaign
items.summary: March 2026 phishing attack compromised an airline booking software provider's IT admin credentials via MFA fatigue, exposing cloud and OT systems.

items.title: ICAO 2026–2028 Global Aviation Security Plan Targets Weaponised UAS and GNSS Threats
items.summary: ICAO Assembly adopted the Second Edition Global Aviation Security Plan, directing new work programme on weaponised UAS and GNSS radio frequency interference.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Mandate Now Fully Binding Across EU Aviation
items.summary: EASA Part-IS cybersecurity regulation became fully applicable as of February 2026, covering airlines, airports, MROs and ATC with mandatory ISMS requirements.

days.briefing: Global aviation security faces a high-tempo threat environment driven by escalating GPS/GNSS spoofing campaigns targeting multiple countries, a sustained wave of cyber intrusions against airline and airport IT infrastructure, and fresh physical disruptions to critical airport facilities. Regulatory bodies including EASA, FAA, and ICAO are actively issuing conflict-zone bulletins, UAS restriction frameworks, and mandatory cybersecurity compliance deadlines in response to these converging threats.

items.title: Caracas Simón Bolívar Airport Shut Down After Earthquake Destroys Terminal
items.summary: Twin earthquakes caused severe structural damage to Venezuela's main international gateway, suspending all flights pending full safety inspections of screening areas and infrastructure.

items.title: EASA Issues New Conflict Zone Bulletin on Iran, Iraq and Lebanon Airspace
items.summary: EASA CZIB 2026-03-R12 directs operators to avoid Iran, Iraq and Lebanon airspace and exercise caution across Bahrain, Israel, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, Oman and Saudi Arabia.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM to Restrict Drone Flights Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM establishes a process for facility owners to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comment period closes 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Hit by MFA-Fatigue Phishing Attack Affecting Multiple Airlines
items.summary: Threat actors used social engineering and MFA fatigue to compromise an IT administrator's credentials, gaining access to identity systems, Microsoft 365 and OT environments.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Implementing Regulation Now Fully Applicable
items.summary: EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity rule became fully applicable on 22 February 2026, mandating Information Security Management Systems for airlines, airports, MROs and air navigation service providers.

items.title: India Confirms Coordinated GPS Spoofing Campaign Targeting Eight Major Airports
items.summary: India's Civil Aviation Minister confirmed GPS spoofing and GNSS jamming at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Amritsar, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Chennai airports; source unattributed, state-sponsored actor suspected.

days.briefing: The global aviation security environment over the past 72 hours has been marked by a deadly JNIM terrorist assault on Niamey's international airport in Niger and confirmed GPS spoofing attacks across seven major Indian airports, underscoring persistent physical and cyber threats to civil aviation worldwide. Simultaneously, the EU Council circulated a new Aviation Security Strategy document on 19 June 2026, and the FAA's critical infrastructure drone-restriction rulemaking (NPRM) entered its public-comment phase, reflecting accelerating regulatory responses to evolving UAS and hybrid threats.

items.title: JNIM Militants Storm Niamey International Airport; 13 Killed
items.summary: Al-Qaeda-linked JNIM attacked Diori Hamani Airport on 18 Jun 2026, killing 13; 22 attackers killed, 20 arrested. Second airport attack in 2026.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad and Bengaluru airports, feeding false navigation data to approaching aircraft; no flights cancelled.

items.title: EU Council Circulates New Aviation Security Strategy Document
items.summary: Council document COM(2026)229 dated 19 Jun proposes faster classified-information sharing, cargo supply-chain hardening, and a conflict-zone risk-assessment mechanism with EASA/EEAS.

items.title: FAA Publishes NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA published long-awaited Section 2209 NPRM on 6 May 2026 to allow facility operators to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: FAA Reports 100+ Monthly Drone Incursions Near Airports Amid World Cup Security Pressure
items.summary: FAA receives over 100 monthly drone sighting reports near airports; experts warn counter-UAS readiness remains uneven across federal, state and local agencies ahead of FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Korean Air Data Breach Exposes 30,000 Employee Records via Oracle EBS Campaign
items.summary: Hackers exploited Oracle EBS zero-days to steal Korean Air employee names and bank details from catering subsidiary KC&D; Envoy Air also confirmed victim of same campaign.

days.briefing: Rogue drone activity at airports and major events, alongside a surge in ransomware attacks on aviation supply chains, are the most pressing global AVSEC threats; new EASA Part-IS regulations aim to bolster cybersecurity resilience.

items.title: Hundreds of Drones Seized at FIFA World Cup 2026
items.summary: Over 400 drones seized from restricted airspace across 11 US host cities; TSA reports 300+ seizures.
items.fullContent: Over 400 unmanned aircraft systems (UAS), commonly known as drones, were seized in restricted airspace across 11 US host cities during the FIFA World Cup 2026, as reported by KFOX-TV on June 17, 2026. The Transportation Security Administration (TSA) confirmed that more than 300 of these seizures occurred within Temporary Flight Restrictions (TFRs) established around stadium venues. The operations, which began with the tournament’s opening matches in June, involved federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies, including the Department of Homeland Security, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), and municipal police departments. The scale of the incident is notable, with the 400+ seizures representing the largest single-event drone interdiction effort in US history, though no specific timeline for individual confiscations was provided. The impact on airspace security was immediate, as each incursion required the temporary closure of airspace or the deployment of counter-UAS systems, such as radio frequency jammers and detection radars, to neutralize the threat. Technically, many of the seized drones were consumer-grade quadcopters, but a small number were identified as modified or commercial models capable of carrying payloads, raising concerns about potential espionage or malicious use. Official responses included TSA and FAA joint statements emphasizing zero tolerance for TFR violations, with civil penalties of up to $30,000 per incident and potential criminal referrals. For aviation security, this development underscores the persistent vulnerability of major sporting events to unauthorized drone incursions, highlighting the need for enhanced real-time detection, interagency coordination, and stricter pre-event public awareness campaigns to deter non-compliant operators.

items.title: Ransomware Attack on Collins Aerospace Disrupts European Flights
items.summary: Cyberattack on Collins Aerospace systems caused major delays at London Heathrow and other European airports.
items.fullContent: A targeted ransomware attack against Collins Aerospace systems on June 15-16, 2026, caused significant operational disruption across several major European airports, with London Heathrow bearing the brunt of the cascade failures. Collins Aerospace, a key supplier of flight management and avionics systems for the aviation sector, is the primary entity involved, with reports initially surfacing via LinkedIn from aviation security specialist Rob Demain. The timeline indicates the initial breach began late on June 15, with critical systems degrading overnight and full impact manifesting by the morning of June 16. The scale was severe: at Heathrow, dozens of flights were delayed or cancelled as air traffic control and airline dispatch systems reliant on Collins-supplied software experienced failures, with ripple effects reported at Paris Charles de Gaulle and Frankfurt. Technically, the attack appears to have involved a sophisticated ransomware variant that encrypted critical flight planning and maintenance databases, exploiting a known vulnerability in remote access protocols. Official response included the immediate grounding of affected aircraft, activation of manual flight planning procedures by airlines, and the deployment of the UK National Cyber Security Centre and Europol’s European Cybercrime Centre to assist Collins’ internal incident response teams. The implications for aviation security are profound: this event underscores the systemic fragility inherent in the concentration of critical aviation software among a small number of suppliers, demonstrating that a single cyberattack on a Tier 1 vendor can paralyze cross-border operations. It highlights an urgent need for regulatory mandates requiring air-gapped backup systems and real-time threat sharing between aviation software providers and national security agencies, as well as a fundamental reassessment of supply chain cyber resilience standards across the European aviation ecosystem.

items.title: 131% Surge in Aviation Cyberattacks in 2026
items.summary: Cyberattacks on aviation up 131%, with threat actors hiding for over 300 days; ransomware and nation-state threats cited.
items.fullContent: Analysis of the 2026 surge in aviation cyberattacks reveals a 131% increase in incidents targeting the sector compared to the previous year, as reported by the cybersecurity firm Polyswarm via its Instagram channel on May 1, 2026. The data indicates that threat actors maintained prolonged undetected access, averaging over 300 days within compromised networks before execution of malicious actions. This timeline suggests a shift toward highly sophisticated, low-and-slow intrusion tactics, likely involving initial access through phishing campaigns or unpatched vulnerabilities in third-party software. The scale of the surge implicates both ransomware groups, seeking substantial financial payouts by encrypting critical operational data, and nation-state actors, possibly aiming to destabilize aviation infrastructure or conduct espionage. Geographically, North America bore the brunt of the attacks, affecting major airlines, airport operators, and air traffic control ancillary systems. Technical details point to exploitation of identity management flaws, enabling lateral movement across network segments controlling flight dispatch and passenger processing systems. Official responses from the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) have included the issuance of emergency directives mandating multi-factor authentication and network segmentation for all aviation sector partners. The implications for aviation security are severe: the extended dwell time prior to detection underscores a critical gap in continuous monitoring and threat hunting capabilities. Consequently, AVSEC must now prioritize cyber hygiene as a core component of physical security integration, given that a successful cyberattack on reservation systems or flight management databases could directly disrupt flight operations, erode public trust, and provide a vector for physical sabotage. The industry must accelerate adoption of zero-trust architectures and real-time threat intelligence sharing to counter this evolving, persistent threat.

items.title: Rogue Drone Patterns Near Airports Increasing
items.summary: Rogue drone activity evolving into repeatable patterns at airports and correctional facilities, posing flight safety risks.
items.fullContent: On May 1, 2026, D-Fend Solutions, a leading counter-drone technology firm, reported a significant escalation in rogue drone activity across North America, with a notable shift toward repeatable, systematic intrusion patterns at airports and correctional facilities. This analysis indicates that the threat is no longer characterized by random, isolated incidents but by coordinated, potentially hostile operations. The timeline of these events has accelerated over the past quarter, with multiple incidents occurring weekly, increasing in frequency and sophistication. The scale of the impact is substantial, directly compromising flight safety through near-miss events with commercial aircraft and causing operational disruptions, including runway closures and delayed departures. Technically, the drones involved are primarily consumer-grade quadcopters, often modified with longer-range transmitters or autonomous flight controllers to evade geofencing and standard detection. These units execute predefined flight paths, suggesting pre-mission planning and a high degree of operator skill. Official responses have been fragmented, with airport authorities, the FAA, and DHS struggling to implement non-kinetic countermeasures that can safely neutralize the threat without endangering aircraft or personnel. Some airports have begun trialing radio frequency (RF) jamming and spoofing systems, but regulatory hurdles persist. The immediate implications for aviation security are profound: the normalization of repeatable drone patterns demands an urgent, standardized federal response, enhanced collaboration between law enforcement and airport operators, and accelerated deployment of reliable detect-and-defeat systems. Failure to address this trend will invite more brazen attacks, potentially leading to catastrophic loss of life and severe economic damage to the aviation sector.

items.title: EU Part-IS Cybersecurity Regulation Takes Effect
items.summary: EASA Part-IS sets new information security obligations for civil aviation, aligning with NIS 2 Directive.
items.fullContent: The EU Part-IS (Information Security) Regulation, formally adopted by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA), has taken full effect as of 22 February 2026, fundamentally recasting the cybersecurity obligations for civil aviation within the European Union. This regulation, which involved extensive consultation between EASA, the European Commission, and national aviation authorities, directly aligns with the broader Network and Information Security (NIS 2) Directive, creating a cohesive regulatory framework across critical sectors. The timeline for this development saw the publication of the implementing rules in 2023, with a phased compliance period culminating in mandatory enforcement beginning this date. The scale is substantial, impacting all organisations within the aviation ecosystem—including airlines, airports, maintenance organisations, air traffic management providers, and design/production entities—regardless of size, marking a significant expansion over previous, more sector-specific guidance. Technically, Part-IS mandates a risk-based, multi-layered approach to information security management, requiring entities to implement a formal Information Security Management System (ISMS) that covers not only traditional IT systems but also operational technology (OT) for aircraft and ground infrastructure, including incident detection, response, and mandatory reporting to competent national authorities within defined timeframes. The official response from EASA has emphasised that this regulation closes a critical gap, moving from voluntary best practices to binding obligations that criminalise negligence and allow for substantial administrative fines. For aviation security analysts, the immediate implication is a paradigm shift: cybersecurity is no longer a separate, siloed function but an integral, auditable component of overall aviation safety and security (AVSEC). This convergence forces entities to harmonise their security protocols against physical threats with their cybersecurity defences, increasing operational resilience but also raising the technical and financial burden of compliance, particularly for smaller operators.

items.title: Middle East Ceasefire Breaches and UAS Threats to European Airspace
items.summary: Continued ceasefire violations in the Middle East and expanding UAS threats into European airspace impacting civil aviation.
items.fullContent: On 1 June 2026, MedAire reported continued ceasefire violations in the Middle East coupled with an expanding threat from Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) encroaching into European airspace, directly impacting civil aviation operations. The primary actors involved include non-state militant groups operating from conflict zones in the Levant and the broader Middle East, alongside state-backed proxies leveraging this instability. The timeline indicates a sustained escalation over the preceding 72 hours, with multiple breaches of the existing ceasefire framework occurring near key urban centers and transit corridors. The scale of the threat is significant, as UAS incursions have now been detected over sovereign European airspace, particularly over the Eastern Mediterranean and adjacent air traffic control regions, affecting international flight routes to and from major hubs. These incursions pose a direct risk of mid-air collisions and necessitate immediate airspace closures or rerouting, causing cascading delays. Technically, the UAS involved range from commercial off-the-shelf quadcopters used for surveillance to more sophisticated fixed-wing drones capable of extended range and loitering, some reportedly employing GPS spoofing to evade detection. The official response has seen European air navigation service providers, in coordination with Eurocontrol, activate heightened surveillance protocols and issue Notam’s restricting flight levels in affected sectors. National authorities have mobilized counter-UAS systems at major airports, though interoperability remains a challenge. For aviation security, this development underscores a critical vulnerability in the airspace continuum, where a regional ceasefire collapse can directly project kinetic threats into Europe. It mandates an urgent reassessment of risk-based security measures, including enhanced real-time intelligence sharing between Middle Eastern and European agencies, the expedited deployment of C-UAS technology, and the incorporation of drone incursion scenarios into contingency planning for all overflying carriers.
